one_line_takeaway: A quantitative-finance backtesting-methodology paper that repurposes membership-inference-attack scores as a diagnostic for LLM training-data memorization in trading signals, not a security paper -- its headline Cohen's d=18.57 is ~96% a trivial temporal-cutoff artifact, and its central portfolio-level Sharpe improvement (CMMD 4.11 vs Raw Alpha 2.76) falls just short of conventional two-sided significance (p=0.054) over a short 93-day test window.
key_finding: MCS achieves Cohen's d = 18.57, but the paper's own ablation (Section 5.8) shows this composite figure is ~96% attributable to the deterministic temporal-proximity feature alone (d=17.8, requires no fitting), not to the MIA methodology; MIA features alone contribute only d=0.39-1.37 per method (best individual method d=1.68 cited in the contributions list, though Table 3's maximum reported per-model/per-method value is d=-1.37 for TinyLlama loss). Despite this, the MIA-derived cross-model variation is what CMMD actually needs and exploits (temporal proximity alone cannot distinguish models at the same date). CMMD achieved a Sharpe ratio of 4.11 vs. 2.76 for unfiltered Raw Alpha (49% improvement), with clean-model consensus signals producing 14.48 bps average daily return vs. 2.13 bps for tainted signals (7x difference, t=4.82, p<0.001 across 4,650 stock-date pairs). However, the headline CMMD-vs-Raw-Alpha portfolio-level Sharpe difference itself was NOT conventionally significant: two-sided bootstrap p=0.054 (one-sided p=0.027) over only 93 trading days, and the authors explicitly flag this as underpowered (post-hoc analysis suggests ~250 trading days needed for 80% power). Naive threshold-based debiasing (removing signals above median MCS) performed worse than doing nothing at all -- Sharpe 1.54 vs. Raw Alpha's 2.76, a statistically indistinguishable-from-negative bootstrap difference (p=0.91) -- because it indiscriminately discards directionally-correct memorized signals and concentrates the remaining portfolio. Passive Equal-Weight Buy-and-Hold actually had a higher raw Sharpe (5.60) than CMMD (4.11), which the authors attribute to CMMD's long-only market-beta exposure rather than treating it as a failure, arguing CMMD-vs-Raw-Alpha is the fair comparison since both share equivalent beta exposure. The signal-accuracy crossover (in-sample accuracy 40.8%->52.5% rising with contamination quintile Q1->Q5, while out-of-sample accuracy falls 47%->42% over the same quintiles, crossing near Q3) is presented as the clearest direct evidence that memorization inflates apparent quality while degrading generalization.
core_contribution: MemGuard-Alpha is a two-part framework for detecting and neutralizing memorization-contaminated LLM signals in quantitative finance backtesting, not a security/attack paper. (1) MemGuard Composite Score (MCS): a logistic-regression fusion of five membership-inference-attack (MIA) statistics -- loss, Min-K%, Min-K%++, zlib ratio, and reference-model ratio -- plus a deterministic temporal-proximity feature tau(t,c_k), producing a calibrated per-signal contamination probability. (2) Cross-Model Memorization Disagreement (CMMD): the paper's central novel algorithm, which exploits the fact that different LLMs have different training-data cutoff dates, splits models into a 'clean' vs 'tainted' group via a median split on MCS for each (stock, date) pair, and trades only the clean-group consensus signal, formalized by the authors as analogous to a Fuzzy Regression-Discontinuity Design using cutoff-date variation as an exogenous instrument. The authors position this as a fourth mitigation category (post-generation, inference-time signal filtering) distinct from three prior categories they survey: retraining-based prevention (Time Machine GPT, ChronoBERT/ChronoGPT, PiT-Inference), anonymization (entity-neutering, BlindTrade), and divergence decoding.
threat_model: N/A -- this is not a security paper. There is no adversary, no attacker capability, and no system being attacked or defended. MIA, a technique originally developed to infer training-set membership as a privacy attack (Carlini et al., Shi et al. Min-K%, Zhang et al. Min-K%++), is repurposed here purely as a diagnostic/measurement tool: it is used to detect whether an LLM's financial forecast for a given (ticker, date) prompt is likely regurgitated from memorized training data (producing spurious backtest 'alpha') versus genuinely reasoned. The paper's concern is backtest validity and look-ahead bias in quantitative finance, not confidentiality, integrity, or availability of a system, and it proposes no adversary-facing countermeasure -- the 'threat' being defended against is methodological (survivorship/look-ahead bias inflating apparent trading performance), not adversarial.
limitations: The authors themselves list: (1) only 93 trading days give limited portfolio-level statistical power (est. ~250 days needed for 80% power); (2) models tested cap at 7B parameters, so frontier-scale (70B+) memorization patterns are unvalidated; (3) alpha signals use structured prompting on base/small models rather than fine-tuned deployment-representative models; (4) MCS is trained on the same evaluation data (partially mitigated by the temporal feature being a fixed deterministic formula and CMMD depending only on relative MCS ordering, not calibration); (5) the 2019-2024 evaluation window is predominantly bullish, potentially favoring strategies (like CMMD) that preserve bullish signals; (6) reliance on model-card training cutoffs as ground truth, which are approximate; (7) results are confined to 50 large-cap US equities (large caps are disproportionately represented in training data, so effects may not generalize to small-cap/international/other asset classes); (8) survivorship bias from using current (not historical) S&P 100 membership, though the authors argue this affects all six compared strategies equally and so shouldn't differentially favor CMMD; (9) the reference-model MIA method uses a single reference (GPT-2 base) which could itself introduce bias correlated with target-model familiarity.

datasets_used: Yahoo Finance OHLCV daily prices, 50 S&P 100 constituents, Jan 2019-Jun 2024 (~1,375 trading days/stock)
Financial PhraseBank corpus (3,100 human-annotated financial news sentences, pre-2016) used as a guaranteed-memorized reference group
42,800 prompts / 299,600 prompt-model pairs for MIA scoring across 7 models; ~5,900 prompts/model (93 trading days) for alpha-signal generation and portfolio backtesting
quantitative_results: MCS composite Cohen's d = 18.57 (of which temporal-proximity feature alone accounts for d = 17.8, ~96%)
MIA-features-alone Cohen's d range = 0.39-1.37 across models/methods; strongest per-model separation TinyLlama mia_loss d=-1.37 (p<1e-300)
CMMD Sharpe ratio 4.11 vs. Raw Alpha Sharpe 2.76 (49% improvement); bootstrap two-sided p=0.054, one-sided p=0.027
Debiased Alpha (naive threshold) Sharpe 1.54, underperforms Raw Alpha (bootstrap p=0.91, i.e. not significantly different from doing worse)
Equal-Weight Buy-and-Hold Sharpe 5.60 (highest of all strategies, driven by market beta); Momentum-20d Sharpe -0.86; Random Sharpe -0.23
Clean-model signals: 14.48 bps avg daily return vs. tainted-model signals 2.13 bps (7x difference, t=4.82, p<0.001, n=4,650 stock-date pairs)
In-sample signal accuracy by contamination quintile: 40.8% (Q1) to 52.5% (Q5); out-of-sample: 47% (Q2) down to 42% (Q5), crossover near Q3
GPT-2 family MIA separation scales with size: d=-0.19 (124M), -0.26 (355M), -0.33 (774M) on mia_loss
4-bit quantized vs full-precision Qwen 3B MIA-score correlation: Pearson r=0.997 (n=1,000 prompts)
CMMD-Raw Alpha return correlation 0.984; total MIA scoring cost ~4.5 hours for 299,600 pairs on one RTX 5090 (~54 microseconds/pair); full pipeline overhead ~1.15x cost of unfiltered generation
Leave-one-model-out ablation: CMMD Sharpe ranges 3.72 (Phi-2 excluded) to 4.28 (Qwen 7B excluded) vs. 4.11 with all 7 models
baselines_compared: Raw Alpha (unfiltered mean signal across all 7 models)
Single-best-individual-MIA-method variant and MIA-only / temporal-only MCS ablations (internal ablation baselines, not external prior systems)
